name: SAT Prep
description: August 2026 Digital SAT — Math and Reading & Writing. Study rhythm, official practice, and strategy inside Learn v2. Pair with Khan Academy SAT and Bluebook.

nodes.name: Your SAT Game Plan
nodes.description: How the Digital SAT works, what a realistic August timeline looks like when you work a job, and how to use Learn v2 + Khan + Bluebook together without burning out.
keyConcepts: Digital SAT structure: 2 modules Math, 2 modules Reading & Writing
Adaptive testing — second module difficulty depends on first
Score range 400–1600 (200–800 per section)
Consistency beats cramming: 5 days/week, 45–90 min
Official practice > random YouTube drills
nodes.whyItMatters: You are rebuilding years of school habits on your own. A clear plan turns anxiety into a schedule. Colleges care about your score, but they also care that you showed up — this module is your command center.
practiceProblems: Write your test date and work schedule for the next 7 days. Block 3 study slots.
List what you will do on a "bad day" (minimum 20 min: 10 vocab, 10 math problems).
Register or confirm Khan Academy is linked to your College Board account.

resources.title: College Board — SAT Suite
resources.whyHelpful: Official structure, dates, and score info.

resources.title: Bluebook App
resources.whyHelpful: Official digital practice tests — same interface as test day.

resources.title: Khan Academy — SAT Prep
resources.whyHelpful: Free personalized plan synced to College Board.

quiz.question: The Digital SAT total score combines:
quiz.explanation: Total score is 400–1600 from two section scores, each 200–800.

quiz.question: The best source for full-length practice tests is:
quiz.explanation: Bluebook uses the real digital format and official items.

nodes.name: Daily Rhythm & Tools
nodes.description: Set up your study stack: Learn v2 for lessons and notes, Khan for skill drills, Bluebook for timed sections. Learn when to study Math vs R&W.
keyConcepts: Alternate Math and R&W days when possible
One timed section per week by month 2
Track misses in a mistake log (topic, not just "wrong")
Sleep and show-up matter as much as one more hour
nodes.whyItMatters: Discipline from your job already proves you can repeat hard tasks. SAT prep is the same skill — scheduled reps — applied to reading and math.
practiceProblems: Create a mistake log template: date | section | topic | what went wrong | fix.
Pick Mon/Wed/Fri = Math, Tue/Thu = R&W (adjust to your work shifts).

resources.title: Khan — Link to College Board
resources.whyHelpful: Syncs diagnostic scores to a personalized plan.

quiz.question: A sustainable week while working often looks like:
options: Skipping sleep to cram the night before
Most days 45–90 min, one rest day, one timed block weekly
quiz.explanation: Consistency with recovery beats heroic cram sessions.

nodes.name: SAT Math — What They Test
nodes.description: Overview of Digital SAT Math: Algebra, Problem-Solving & Data Analysis, Advanced Math, and Geometry/Trig. Know the terrain before drilling.
keyConcepts: Linear equations, systems, inequalities
Ratios, percentages, units, scatterplots
Quadratics, exponentials, functions
Geometry, angles, circles (lighter weight than algebra)
nodes.whyItMatters: You love complexity but feel behind in class math. SAT math is narrower than "all of school math" — targeted practice can move your score fast.
practiceProblems: Rate yourself 1–5 on: linear equations, fractions, word problems, graphs.
Do 5 Khan questions in your weakest rated topic.

resources.title: Khan — SAT Math
resources.whyHelpful: Topic-sorted official-style practice.

resources.title: Learn v2 — Mathematics subject
resources.whyHelpful: Rebuild foundations (m1–m5) in parallel for deep understanding.

quiz.question: Which topic appears most often on SAT Math?
quiz.explanation: Algebra and problem-solving dominate; calculus is not on the SAT.

nodes.name: Algebra & Linear Equations
nodes.description: Solve for x, translate word problems, work with slopes and systems. The highest-yield math bucket for most students.
keyConcepts: Isolate the variable — same operation both sides
y = mx + b: slope and intercept meaning
Systems: substitution and elimination
Translate words → equation (what is x?)
nodes.whyItMatters: Many missed school days show up here. Fixing algebra unlocks dozens of SAT questions and builds confidence for harder topics.
practiceProblems: Solve: 3x + 7 = 22. Then: 2x - 5 = 3x + 1.
A phone plan is $25 plus $0.10 per minute. Write the equation and cost for 40 minutes.
Khan: 15 algebra questions — log any topic you miss twice.

resources.title: Khan — Linear equations
resources.whyHelpful: Step-by-step if you are catching up from scratch.

quiz.question: Solve: 2(x - 3) = 10
In y = 3x + 2, the 3 represents:
quiz.explanation: In y = mx + b, m is slope (rate of change).

nodes.name: Problem Solving & Data
nodes.description: Percentages, ratios, proportional reasoning, mean/median, tables and scatterplots. SAT loves real-world word problems.
keyConcepts: Percent change: (new - old) / old
Unit conversion and dimensional analysis
Reading tables and graphs carefully
Mean vs median — which outlier affects which
nodes.whyItMatters: These questions reward careful reading — similar to trading risk/reward thinking. Good fit for how your brain likes real scenarios.
practiceProblems: A $40 item is 25% off. What is the sale price?
From a table in Khan practice, identify the median of 5 values.
10 Khan problem-solving & data questions timed (2 min each max).

resources.title: Khan — Percent & ratios
resources.whyHelpful: Core drill for word-problem style items.

quiz.question: Price rises from $80 to $100. Percent increase?
quiz.explanation: Increase 20/80 = 25%.

nodes.name: Advanced Math Essentials
nodes.description: Quadratics, factoring basics, function notation, and exponentials at SAT level — not full precalculus.
keyConcepts: f(x) notation — input and output
Factoring x² + bx + c
Quadratic formula when factoring fails
Exponential growth vs linear growth
nodes.whyItMatters: This is where score gains separate 1100 from 1300+ for many students. You do not need mastery of college math — you need SAT-shaped fluency.
practiceProblems: If f(x) = 2x + 1, find f(3) and f(-2).
Factor: x² - 5x + 6.
Khan: 10 advanced math SAT items.

resources.title: Khan — Quadratic functions
resources.whyHelpful: SAT-level quadratics without full precalc course.

quiz.question: What is f(4) if f(x) = x² - 3?

nodes.name: Math Strategy & Timing
nodes.description: When to skip, when to guess, how to use Desmos on the Digital SAT, and how to check answers without redoing everything.
keyConcepts: Answer easy questions first in each module
Desmos graphing for systems and intersections
Plug in answer choices (backsolving)
Mark and return — do not spiral on one hard item
nodes.whyItMatters: A strong student who runs out of time scores lower than a prepared student who manages pace. Strategy is part of the test.
practiceProblems: Take one Bluebook Math module untimed — note where you stalled.
Retry missed questions using backsolving on multiple choice only.

resources.title: Bluebook — Practice Tests
resources.whyHelpful: Built-in Desmos matches test day.

quiz.question: You are stuck on a multiple-choice algebra question after 2 minutes. Best move:
options: Eliminate wrong choices, guess, mark for review if time
quiz.explanation: No penalty for wrong answers on SAT — educated guesses are +EV.

nodes.name: Math — Timed Module Practice
nodes.description: Run a timed Math module (or half test) in Bluebook. Log errors by topic in your mistake log. Review in Learn v2 notes.
keyConcepts: Simulate test conditions: timer on, phone away
Review every miss within 24 hours
Redo 3 similar Khan questions per miss type
nodes.whyItMatters: This is the rehearsal before showtime. Timed practice reveals gaps content study hides.
practiceProblems: Complete Bluebook Math Module 1 (or Khan timed set).
Write 3 takeaways in lesson notes for your worst topic.

resources.title: Bluebook timed practice
resources.whyHelpful: Closest thing to test day.

quiz.question: After a timed Math module, the highest-ROI review is:
options: Categorizing misses and drilling those topics
quiz.explanation: Topic-level review prevents repeating the same miss.

nodes.name: Reading & Writing — Overview
nodes.description: How SAT R&W works: short passages, one question each, grammar and reading skills combined. Build reading stamina without zoning out.
keyConcepts: Passages are short — pace is ~1 min per question
Question types: grammar, vocabulary in context, main idea, evidence
Read the question stem first on hard items
Wrong answers often half-right — find the flaw
nodes.whyItMatters: You are extroverted and like people — R&W is about careful communication, not "being a literature kid." Treat it like debugging text.
practiceProblems: Read one SAT passage slowly; summarize the main idea in one sentence.
Khan: 10 R&W questions untimed — label each question type.

resources.title: Khan — SAT Reading & Writing
resources.whyHelpful: Official-style R&W drill.

quiz.question: On SAT R&W, most questions ask you to:
options: Fix grammar or interpret a short passage
quiz.explanation: Digital SAT R&W is passage-based multiple choice, including Standard English conventions.

nodes.name: Grammar & Standard English
nodes.description: Subject-verb agreement, pronouns, commas, sentence boundaries, and concise phrasing. Fast points when you know the rules.
keyConcepts: Subject-verb agreement (singular/plural)
Comma splices and run-ons
Who vs whom, they vs them (SAT tests common cases)
Shorter is often correct when grammar is equal
nodes.whyItMatters: Grammar questions are the most trainable part of R&W — less background knowledge than history passages.
practiceProblems: Fix: "The team are winning, however they played sloppy."
Khan: 15 Standard English convention questions.

resources.title: Khan — Grammar
resources.whyHelpful: Rule-based drill with explanations.

quiz.question: Choose the best fix: "Neither the students nor the teacher were ready."
quiz.explanation: With neither/nor, verb agrees with nearer subject (teacher → was).

nodes.name: Words in Context & Craft
nodes.description: Vocabulary in context, tone, purpose, and structure questions. Eliminate answers that change the passage's meaning.
keyConcepts: Context clues beat memorizing 5000 word lists
Author's purpose: inform, argue, describe, etc.
Transition words signal logic between sentences
Dual passages: compare two authors' views
nodes.whyItMatters: These feel subjective but are logical — like reading market sentiment vs price. Practice builds pattern recognition.
practiceProblems: For one Khan passage, write why each wrong answer fails in 5 words.
Learn 5 high-frequency words only from missed questions.

resources.title: Khan — Craft and structure
resources.whyHelpful: Craft and structure question bank.

quiz.question: For "words in context" questions, the best first step is:
options: Reread the sentence and predict your own word
quiz.explanation: Prediction before looking at choices reduces trap answers.

nodes.name: Information, Ideas & Evidence
nodes.description: Main idea, detail, inference, and paired evidence questions. Learn to prove answers with lines from the text.
keyConcepts: Main idea ≠ one detail
Evidence pairs: pick the quote that supports your answer
Inference must stay close to the text — no outside assumptions
Charts in R&W: read axes and units first
nodes.whyItMatters: Evidence questions reward discipline — same as citing reasons in an argument. Good match for someone who values honesty and clarity.
practiceProblems: One passage: answer main idea, then find one sentence that proves it.
Khan: 10 Information and Ideas questions.

resources.title: Khan — Information and ideas
resources.whyHelpful: Evidence and inference drill.

quiz.question: An inference answer should be:
options: Strongly supported by the passage, not extreme
Based on outside history knowledge only
quiz.explanation: SAT rewards conservative, text-bound inferences.

nodes.name: R&W Strategy & Pacing
nodes.description: Module timing, when to skip, handling boring passages, and staying sharp after Math sections on test day.
keyConcepts: ~1 minute per question average — some faster, some slower
If zoning out, physically underline the question stem
Grammar items often faster than long inference items
Full test day: energy management (snack, sleep)
nodes.whyItMatters: R&W is where many students lose points to fatigue, not ability. Pacing practice is score practice.
practiceProblems: Timed: 10 R&W questions in 12 minutes.
Plan test-day breakfast and arrival time (30 min early).

resources.title: College Board — What to Bring
resources.whyHelpful: ID, device, admission ticket checklist.

quiz.question: During R&W, if a passage is boring, best tactic:
options: Underline key phrases and answer the specific question
quiz.explanation: You only need enough of the passage to answer the asked question.

nodes.name: R&W — Timed Module Practice
nodes.description: Timed R&W module in Bluebook or Khan. Log misses. Tie weak areas back to grammar vs reading lessons in this tree.
keyConcepts: Timed module under test-like conditions
Tag misses: grammar / vocab / main idea / evidence
Review within 24 hours
nodes.whyItMatters: Pairs with Math timed practice — together they predict how August will feel.
practiceProblems: Bluebook R&W Module 1 or equivalent timed set.
Three bullet notes on what to drill next week.

resources.title: Bluebook
resources.whyHelpful: Official timing and interface.

quiz.question: After R&W timed practice, prioritize review of:
options: Every miss, by category, with one drill each
quiz.explanation: Categorized review is how R&W scores climb steadily.

nodes.name: Full Practice Test & Test Week
nodes.description: Take a full Digital SAT in Bluebook. Build a one-week test plan: sleep, materials, score send strategy, and what to do the morning of.
keyConcepts: Full test = stamina + strategy + content
Score is a snapshot — one test is not your whole story
Send scores to colleges that fit your aid profile
Day before: light review, materials packed, early sleep
nodes.whyItMatters: This is the finish line of the module — and the start of applications. You registered and paid; now you execute.
practiceProblems: Schedule your Bluebook full practice test date this week.
List 3 colleges to research for fit + financial aid (start broad).
Write a 3-sentence "why I'm taking this test" for yourself — motivation anchor.

resources.title: BigFuture — College Search
resources.whyHelpful: Explore schools and majors alongside SAT prep.

resources.title: FAFSA overview
resources.whyHelpful: Financial aid starts here for low-income families.

quiz.question: The night before the SAT, you should:
options: Take the test without practicing once
quiz.explanation: Rest and logistics beat last-minute cramming.

quiz.question: A full practice test is most useful when you:
options: Review every section and adjust your study plan
quiz.explanation: Review drives the next week of study.

nodes.name: Fractions & Rational Numbers
nodes.description: Focused drill on fraction operations, equivalence, and SAT word problems with rational expressions. Build speed without skipping steps.
keyConcepts: Add/subtract fractions — common denominator first
Multiply and divide — flip and simplify
Convert between fractions, decimals, and percents
Rational expressions: factor before canceling
nodes.whyItMatters: Fraction mistakes are silent score killers — one arithmetic slip on an otherwise easy item. This drill locks in the habits that show up across algebra and data questions.
practiceProblems: A recipe uses 3/4 cup flour per batch. How much for 2.5 batches?
Khan: 15 fraction and rational-number SAT items — log any repeat miss.

resources.title: Khan — Fractions & rational numbers
resources.whyHelpful: Step-by-step fraction drills with SAT-style word problems.

resources.title: Learn v2 — Mathematics subject
resources.whyHelpful: Rebuild pre-algebra foundations (m1–m3) if fractions still feel shaky.

quiz.question: What is 3/4 ÷ 1/2?
quiz.explanation: Dividing by 1/2 is multiplying by 2: (3/4) × 2 = 3/2.

quiz.question: Which is equivalent to 0.375?

nodes.name: Linear Equations — Focused Drill
nodes.description: One- and two-step equations, translating words to algebra, and slope-intercept fluency under drill conditions.
keyConcepts: Isolate the variable — same operation both sides
Translate keywords: per, total, twice, difference
y = mx + b: slope as rate, b as starting value
Check solutions by substitution
nodes.whyItMatters: Linear equations are the highest-frequency SAT math topic. Drill speed here pays off on dozens of questions across modules.
practiceProblems: A gym charges $30 plus $5 per visit. Write y = mx + b and find cost for 8 visits.
Khan: 15 linear equation SAT items timed (3 min max each).

resources.title: Khan — Linear equations
resources.whyHelpful: Core equation-solving drill with explanations.

resources.title: Bluebook — Practice Tests
resources.whyHelpful: Official items show how linear questions appear on test day.

quiz.question: Solve: 5x - 3 = 2x + 9
In y = -2x + 7, the y-intercept is:
quiz.explanation: In y = mx + b, b = 7 is the y-intercept.

nodes.name: Systems of Equations Drill
nodes.description: Substitution, elimination, and recognizing when a system has no solution or infinitely many — at SAT pace.
keyConcepts: Substitution when one variable is already isolated
Elimination — align coefficients and add/subtract
Parallel lines → no solution; same line → infinite solutions
Graph intersection as visual check
nodes.whyItMatters: Systems combine two skills you already know — but under time pressure students pick the slow method. Drilling both paths saves minutes per module.
practiceProblems: Solve: y = 2x + 1 and 3x + y = 16.
Solve: 2x + 3y = 12 and 4x + 6y = 24 — how many solutions?
Khan: 12 systems SAT items; try each with substitution and elimination.

resources.title: Khan — Systems of equations
resources.whyHelpful: Substitution and elimination drills with worked solutions.

resources.title: Desmos Graphing Calculator
resources.whyHelpful: Graph both lines to verify intersection points quickly.

quiz.question: Two parallel lines in a system of equations have:
quiz.explanation: Parallel lines never intersect — the system is inconsistent.

quiz.question: For x + y = 10 and x - y = 2, x equals:
quiz.explanation: Add equations: 2x = 12 → x = 6.

nodes.name: Percentages & Proportions Drill
nodes.description: Percent change, ratios, unit rates, and proportional reasoning — the word-problem style SAT loves.
keyConcepts: Percent change: (new - old) / old × 100
Part/whole = percent/100 setup
Unit rates and dimensional analysis
Ratio tables for multi-step problems
nodes.whyItMatters: These questions reward careful reading over advanced math. Nailing proportions is one of the fastest ways to pick up points after a full practice test.
practiceProblems: A $120 jacket is marked down 30%, then an extra 10% off the sale price. Final price?
If 4 workers finish a job in 6 days, how long for 6 workers (same rate)?
Khan: 15 percent and ratio SAT items — tag each miss as setup vs arithmetic.

resources.title: Khan — Percent & ratios
resources.whyHelpful: Word-problem drill for percent change and proportional reasoning.

resources.title: Khan — SAT Math practice
resources.whyHelpful: Problem-solving and data analysis question bank.

quiz.question: Price drops from $50 to $40. Percent decrease?
quiz.explanation: Decrease 10/50 = 20%.

quiz.question: A map scale is 1 inch : 5 miles. 3.5 inches represents:

nodes.name: Quadratics & Factoring Drill
nodes.description: Factoring, completing the square basics, quadratic formula, and parabola features at SAT level.
keyConcepts: Factor x² + bx + c when possible
Quadratic formula when factoring fails
Vertex and axis of symmetry from standard form
Discriminant hints: two, one, or zero real roots
nodes.whyItMatters: Quadratics separate good scores from great ones. You do not need full precalc — you need SAT-shaped fluency with factoring and the formula.
practiceProblems: Factor: x² - 7x + 12.
Solve: x² + 4x - 5 = 0 by factoring and by quadratic formula.
Khan: 12 quadratic SAT items — note when factoring beats the formula.

resources.title: Khan — Quadratic functions
resources.whyHelpful: Factoring and formula drill without full precalc depth.

resources.title: Learn v2 — Mathematics subject
resources.whyHelpful: Deepen algebra foundations in parallel if quadratics feel new.

quiz.question: Factor: x² - 5x + 6
quiz.explanation: Numbers that multiply to 6 and add to -5 are -2 and -3.

quiz.question: For y = x² - 4x + 3, the x-coordinate of the vertex is:
quiz.explanation: Vertex x = -b/(2a) = 4/2 = 2.

nodes.name: Functions & Graphs Drill
nodes.description: Function notation, table/graph matching, intercepts, and interpreting linear and nonlinear graphs on the Digital SAT.
keyConcepts: f(x) notation — input in, output out
Match tables, equations, and graphs
x- and y-intercepts from equation or graph
Compare rates of change on piecewise graphs
nodes.whyItMatters: Graph questions test whether you connect symbolic and visual math. Drilling the translation between forms prevents careless mismatches.
practiceProblems: If f(x) = x² - 2x, find f(3) and f(-1).
From a graph, identify where f(x) = 0 and where f(x) is increasing.
Khan: 12 functions and graphs SAT items — sketch before checking choices.

resources.title: Khan — Functions
resources.whyHelpful: Function notation and graph interpretation drills.

resources.title: Desmos Graphing Calculator
resources.whyHelpful: Plot functions quickly to match tables and intercept questions.

quiz.question: If f(x) = 3x - 1, what is f(5)?
The y-intercept of a graph is where:
quiz.explanation: The y-intercept is the point where the graph crosses the y-axis (x = 0).

nodes.name: Desmos on the Digital SAT
nodes.description: Graphing equations, finding intersections, regression lines, and backsolving with the built-in calculator — strategy drill, not just button tutorial.
keyConcepts: Type equations to graph lines and parabolas instantly
Find intersection points for systems visually
Regression / line of best fit on scatter data
Backsolve by graphing answer choices
nodes.whyItMatters: Desmos is allowed on every Math question. Students who practice it here save time on systems, quadratics, and some data items that others grind by hand.
practiceProblems: Graph y = 2x + 3 and y = -x + 9 — read the intersection coordinates.
Enter a scatter table from one Khan item and fit a regression line.
Retry 3 missed algebra questions from your log using Desmos only.

resources.title: Khan — Desmos SAT tips
resources.whyHelpful: SAT math practice with the same Desmos tool as test day.

resources.title: Desmos — SAT Test Mode
resources.whyHelpful: Practice in the exact College Board graphing calculator interface.

quiz.question: Desmos is most helpful on the SAT for:
quiz.explanation: Desmos shines on graphing, systems, and visual checks — not R&W.

quiz.question: To backsolve a multiple-choice equation, you should:
options: Graph each answer choice and see which fits
quiz.explanation: Graphing answer choices (or the equation vs a constant) is a core Desmos tactic.

nodes.name: Geometry & Right-Triangle Trig
nodes.description: Angles, circles, area and volume, similar triangles, and SOHCAHTOA at SAT level — lighter weight than algebra but still drill-worthy.
keyConcepts: Triangle angle sum and special right triangles (45-45-90, 30-60-90)
Circle: arc length, sector area, central angles
Similar triangles — set up proportions
SOHCAHTOA for right-triangle trig items
nodes.whyItMatters: Geometry questions are fewer but often feel unfamiliar if you missed class days. A focused drill prevents easy points from slipping away.
practiceProblems: A right triangle has legs 5 and 12. Find the hypotenuse and sin(θ) for the angle opposite the side of length 5.
Circle radius 6 — find circumference and area.
Khan: 10 geometry/trig SAT items — draw a diagram for every question.

resources.title: Khan — SAT geometry & trig
resources.whyHelpful: Geometry and right-triangle trig in official SAT style.

resources.title: Learn v2 — Mathematics subject
resources.whyHelpful: Geometry modules (m4+) for deeper angle and triangle review.

quiz.question: In a right triangle, sin(θ) equals:
quiz.explanation: SOH — sine is opposite over hypotenuse.

quiz.question: Two similar triangles have side ratio 2:3. If the smaller has a side of 8, the matching side on the larger is:

nodes.name: Math Mixed Topic Review
nodes.description: Untimed mixed set tagged by topic. Redo misses from your mistake log before the timed checkpoint.
keyConcepts: Mix algebra, data, advanced math, and geometry in one sitting
Tag each miss: topic + error type (setup vs arithmetic vs time)
Redo similar Khan questions for every miss
No new topics — consolidate what st16–st23 covered
nodes.whyItMatters: Single-topic drills build skill; mixed review reveals what you forget when topics blend — exactly what a real module feels like.
practiceProblems: Khan: 20-question untimed mixed Math set spanning all drill topics.
For each miss, write one sentence: what topic and what fix.
Redo the 3 worst misses with a fresh attempt before st25.

resources.title: Khan — SAT Math practice
resources.whyHelpful: Mixed official-style sets with topic labels.

resources.title: Bluebook — Practice Tests
resources.whyHelpful: Untimed module review in the real test interface.

quiz.question: Before a timed checkpoint, mixed review should be:
options: Untimed with every miss tagged by topic
quiz.explanation: Tagged untimed review targets weak spots before the clock turns on.

quiz.question: The best use of a mistake log during mixed review is:
options: Note topic and error pattern for each miss
quiz.explanation: Topic + error pattern drives what to drill next.

nodes.name: Math — Timed Module Checkpoint
nodes.description: Bluebook or Khan timed Math module. Log misses by topic within 24 hours — capstone of the st16–st25 drill chain.
keyConcepts: Timed module under test-like conditions
Tag misses: fractions / linear / systems / percent / quadratics / graphs / geometry
Review within 24 hours — redo 3 similar items per miss type
Compare to st8 timed practice — note score trend
nodes.whyItMatters: This checkpoint proves the drill chain worked. Timed performance after focused review is the feedback loop that moves your Math score.
practiceProblems: Bluebook Math Module or Khan timed set — phone away, timer on.
Categorize every miss in your mistake log within 24 hours.
Pick one weak topic from the log and schedule st16–st23 redo next week.

resources.title: Khan — Timed SAT Math
resources.whyHelpful: Timed sets with instant review and topic tags.

resources.title: Bluebook timed practice
resources.whyHelpful: Official timing, Desmos, and adaptive module feel.

quiz.question: After a timed Math checkpoint, highest-ROI next step is:
options: Log misses by topic and drill each within 24 hours
quiz.explanation: Categorized review within 24 hours locks in fixes while mistakes are fresh.

quiz.question: During a timed Math module, if stuck after ~2 minutes you should:
options: Spend 10 more minutes on one item
Eliminate, guess, mark for review, move on
quiz.explanation: No penalty for guessing — protect time for questions you can solve.

nodes.name: Subject-Verb Agreement Drill
nodes.description: Focused drill on singular/plural subjects, collective nouns, neither/nor constructions, and interrupting phrases that hide the real subject.
keyConcepts: Find the subject — ignore prepositional phrases between subject and verb
Collective nouns (team, group, family) usually take singular verbs
Neither/nor and either/or — verb agrees with the nearer subject
Indefinite pronouns: everyone, each, nobody → singular
nodes.whyItMatters: Agreement errors are fast points on the SAT — one rule applied correctly beats re-reading a whole passage. This drill builds the reflex that catches traps before you pick an answer.
practiceProblems: Fix: "The group of students were excited about the trip."
Fix: "Neither the coach nor the players was ready for overtime."
Khan: 15 subject-verb agreement SAT items — underline the subject on every question.

resources.title: Khan — Subject-verb agreement
resources.whyHelpful: Rule-based agreement drills with SAT-style distractors.

resources.title: Khan — SAT Reading & Writing
resources.whyHelpful: Full R&W bank to mix agreement with other grammar items.

quiz.question: Choose the best fix: "Each of the volunteers have signed up for a shift."
quiz.explanation: Each is singular — the verb must be has, not have.

quiz.question: In "The box of tools are in the garage," the subject is:
quiz.explanation: Ignore the prepositional phrase "of tools" — subject is box (singular → is, not are).

nodes.name: Punctuation & Sentence Boundaries
nodes.description: Commas, semicolons, colons, dashes, run-ons, and comma splices — the punctuation rules the SAT tests repeatedly under time pressure.
keyConcepts: Comma splice: two independent clauses joined only by a comma — fix with period, semicolon, or comma + FANBOYS
Semicolon joins two related independent clauses
Colon introduces a list or explanation after a complete clause
Nonessential clauses get commas; essential clauses do not
nodes.whyItMatters: Punctuation questions reward pattern recognition, not taste. Learn the five or six rules the SAT repeats and you pick up points that reading-heavy items can't give you as quickly.
practiceProblems: Fix the comma splice: "The experiment failed, the team revised the method."
Add correct punctuation: "She brought three items ___ a map a compass and water."
Khan: 15 punctuation and sentence-boundary SAT items timed (45 sec each).

resources.title: Khan — Punctuation
resources.whyHelpful: Comma, semicolon, and colon drills in official SAT style.

resources.title: Bluebook — Practice Tests
resources.whyHelpful: See punctuation items in the real digital interface.

quiz.question: Which correctly fixes a comma splice?
options: Replace the comma with a semicolon between two independent clauses
Add a comma before every and
Use a colon between any two sentences
quiz.explanation: A semicolon (or period, or comma + coordinating conjunction) fixes a comma splice.

quiz.question: A colon is correct when it:
options: Follows a complete independent clause and introduces a list or explanation
Joins a dependent clause to a fragment
quiz.explanation: The clause before the colon must stand alone; what follows explains or lists.

nodes.name: Pronouns & Modifier Placement
nodes.description: Pronoun clarity, case, agreement, and dangling or misplaced modifiers — grammar items that look like reading but follow clear rules.
keyConcepts: Pronoun must refer clearly to one noun — no ambiguous they/it
Who vs whom: who = subject, whom = object
Dangling modifier: opening phrase must attach to the subject that follows
Misplaced modifier: put the word being modified next to what it describes
nodes.whyItMatters: Modifier and pronoun questions punish sloppy reading. One slow pass to match each pronoun to its antecedent saves you from trap answers that sound fine at a glance.
practiceProblems: Fix: "Running to catch the bus, the backpack fell off his shoulder."
Fix: "The teacher gave books to the students that were outdated."
Khan: 15 pronoun and modifier SAT items — name the error type for each miss.

resources.title: Khan — Pronouns and modifiers
resources.whyHelpful: Pronoun clarity and modifier placement with explanations.

resources.title: Khan — SAT Reading & Writing
resources.whyHelpful: Mix modifier items with agreement and punctuation in one session.

quiz.question: "Having finished the report, the meeting was cancelled" is flawed because:
options: The meeting cannot finish a report — dangling modifier
quiz.explanation: The opening phrase must modify the subject that follows — the meeting didn't finish the report.

quiz.question: Choose the clearest sentence:
options: When Maria opened the package, it was damaged.
When Maria opened it, the package was damaged.
When opening the package, it was damaged by Maria.
The package, when Maria opened it, was damaged by it.
quiz.explanation: It clearly refers to the package; the subject performing the action is Maria.

nodes.name: Words in Context — Focused Drill
nodes.description: Predict-then-match vocabulary using surrounding sentence and paragraph clues — no flashcard marathon required.
keyConcepts: Cover the answer choices — predict your own word first
Context clues: contrast (however), cause (therefore), definition in same sentence
Wrong answers often match one clue but contradict another part of the passage
Tone and register: formal passage rejects slang choices
nodes.whyItMatters: Words-in-context items feel like vocabulary tests but are logic puzzles. Prediction before looking at choices cuts trap answers — same discipline as checking a trade thesis against the chart.
practiceProblems: One Khan passage: predict a word for each vocab question before revealing choices.
Write why each wrong answer fails — one phrase per option.
Khan: 15 words-in-context SAT items untimed, then redo misses timed.

resources.title: Khan — Craft and structure
resources.whyHelpful: Words-in-context and tone questions with passage context.

resources.title: Bluebook — Practice Tests
resources.whyHelpful: Official short-passage format matches test day.

quiz.question: The best first step on a "words in context" question is:
options: Reread the sentence and predict your own word before reading choices
quiz.explanation: Prediction anchors you to the passage before distractors pull you off.

quiz.question: A contrast clue like "however" signals the blank should:
options: Oppose or qualify what came before
quiz.explanation: Contrast transitions flip direction — the blank often contrasts with the prior clause.

nodes.name: Main Idea & Central Claims
nodes.description: Distinguish main idea from supporting detail and identify the author's central claim in science and humanities passages.
keyConcepts: Main idea covers the whole passage — not one paragraph or one example
Central claim in argument passages = what the author wants you to believe
Too narrow = one detail; too broad = goes beyond the passage
First and last paragraphs often frame the main idea — verify with middle content
nodes.whyItMatters: Main idea questions reward the same skill as summarizing a news article in one sentence — scope and accuracy. Drill here prevents you from picking answers that are true but too small.
practiceProblems: Read one science passage; write the main idea in 15 words or fewer.
Read one humanities passage; identify the author's central claim in one sentence.
Khan: 10 main-idea SAT items — eliminate too-narrow and too-broad choices first.

resources.title: Khan — Information and ideas
resources.whyHelpful: Main idea and central claim questions across passage types.

resources.title: Khan — SAT Reading & Writing
resources.whyHelpful: Mix science and humanities passages for scope practice.

quiz.question: A main idea answer should be:
options: Broad enough to cover the passage but not beyond it
The opposite of what the author argues
quiz.explanation: Main idea = full scope of the passage — not too narrow, not too broad.

quiz.question: When two answer choices both seem partially true, prefer the one that:
options: Captures what the entire passage is about
quiz.explanation: Main idea must account for the whole passage, not just a supporting detail.

nodes.name: Evidence & Paired Questions
nodes.description: Select the quote or detail that best supports a prior answer — and avoid half-right evidence traps on paired items.
keyConcepts: Answer the first question before looking at evidence choices
Best evidence directly proves your answer — not just related topic
Half-right quotes mention the topic but don't support the specific claim
Line-reference questions: read the full sentence around the cited lines
nodes.whyItMatters: Evidence pairs are the SAT's proof-of-work questions. If you can cite the line that backs your answer, you're thinking like the test — and like someone who shows their reasoning.
practiceProblems: One paired set: answer Q1, then find the line that would convince a skeptic.
For three wrong evidence options, write why each fails to prove the claim.
Khan: 10 evidence and paired-question SAT items.

resources.title: Khan — Information and ideas
resources.whyHelpful: Paired evidence questions with line references.

resources.title: Bluebook — Practice Tests
resources.whyHelpful: Official paired-question format and timing.

quiz.question: On a paired evidence question, you should:
options: Read evidence choices before answering the first question
Answer the first question, then pick evidence that directly supports it
Skip the first question and guess
quiz.explanation: Lock in your answer first — then evidence must prove that specific claim.

quiz.question: A common evidence trap is a quote that:
options: Mentions the topic but doesn't support your specific answer
Comes from the first paragraph only
quiz.explanation: Half-right evidence touches the subject but fails to prove the claim you selected.

nodes.name: Inference & Logical Conclusions
nodes.description: Text-bound inferences only — eliminate extreme or outside-knowledge choices and stay close to what the passage actually says.
keyConcepts: Inference = must be strongly suggested by the text, not merely possible
Extreme words (always, never, completely) are usually wrong
Outside knowledge doesn't help — if it's not in the passage, it's not the answer
Author attitude inference: look at word choice and tone markers
nodes.whyItMatters: Inference questions punish overthinking. The right answer feels modest — it extends the text one small step, not three. Drill builds the habit of asking "where does the passage say this?"
practiceProblems: One passage: for each inference question, underline the exact words that support your pick.
List three trap patterns you fall for (extreme language, outside facts, opposite tone).
Khan: 10 inference SAT items — reject any answer with unsupported extremes.

resources.title: Khan — Information and ideas
resources.whyHelpful: Inference and logical conclusion drills with explanations.

resources.title: Khan — SAT Reading & Writing
resources.whyHelpful: Mix inference with evidence items in one sitting.

quiz.question: A valid SAT inference must be:
options: Strongly supported by the passage without going beyond it
Based on historical facts not in the text
quiz.explanation: Inferences stay conservative and text-bound — no outside assumptions.

quiz.question: Answer choices with "always" or "never" are often wrong because:
options: SAT passages never use those words
They overgeneralize beyond what the text supports
quiz.explanation: Extreme language rarely matches the cautious scope of SAT passages.

nodes.name: Rhetorical Synthesis & Transitions
nodes.description: Bullet notes, logical transitions, and concise revision for Digital SAT synthesis items — combine ideas without losing clarity.
keyConcepts: Synthesis: combine bullet notes into one sentence that meets a specific goal
Read the prompt goal first (emphasize contrast, introduce a finding, etc.)
Transitions: however = contrast, therefore = result, for example = illustration
Concise and correct beats long and vague when grammar is equal
nodes.whyItMatters: Synthesis questions test whether you can communicate clearly under constraints — the same skill as writing a tight update at work. They're trainable once you learn to match the prompt's goal.
practiceProblems: Given three bullet notes, write two different sentences — one emphasizing cause, one emphasizing contrast.
Khan: 10 rhetorical synthesis and transition SAT items.
For each miss, identify whether you failed on goal, grammar, or logic.

resources.title: Khan — Expression of ideas
resources.whyHelpful: Rhetorical synthesis and transition questions in official style.

resources.title: Bluebook — Practice Tests
resources.whyHelpful: Digital SAT synthesis format matches test day.

quiz.question: On a rhetorical synthesis item, the first step is:
options: Combine every bullet into the longest sentence possible
Read what the prompt asks you to accomplish (contrast, emphasize, introduce)
Ignore the bullets and write from memory
Pick the answer with the most commas
quiz.explanation: The goal in the prompt determines which notes to include and how to frame them.

quiz.question: "However" most appropriately signals:
options: A conclusion with no prior setup
quiz.explanation: However introduces contrast — the next idea qualifies or opposes what came before.

nodes.name: R&W Mixed Topic Review
nodes.description: Untimed mixed set across grammar and reading skills. Tag each miss by category before the timed checkpoint.
keyConcepts: Mix grammar (st26–st28) and reading (st29–st33) in one sitting
Tag each miss: agreement / punctuation / pronouns / vocab / main idea / evidence / inference / synthesis
Redo similar Khan questions for every miss
No new topics — consolidate what st26–st33 covered
nodes.whyItMatters: Single-skill drills build accuracy; mixed review reveals what you forget when question types switch — exactly what a real R&W module feels like.
practiceProblems: Khan: 20-question untimed mixed R&W set spanning all drill topics.
For each miss, write one sentence: category + what fix.
Redo the 3 worst misses with a fresh attempt before st35.

resources.title: Khan — SAT Reading & Writing
resources.whyHelpful: Mixed official-style sets with skill labels.

resources.title: Bluebook — Practice Tests
resources.whyHelpful: Untimed module review in the real test interface.

quiz.question: Before a timed R&W checkpoint, mixed review should be:
options: Untimed with every miss tagged by skill category
quiz.explanation: Tagged untimed review targets weak spots before the clock turns on.

quiz.question: The best use of a mistake log during R&W mixed review is:
options: Note skill category and error pattern for each miss
quiz.explanation: Category + error pattern drives what to drill next.

nodes.name: R&W — Timed Module Checkpoint
nodes.description: Bluebook or Khan timed R&W module. Log misses by category within 24 hours — capstone of the st26–st35 drill chain.
keyConcepts: Timed module under test-like conditions
Tag misses: grammar / vocab / main idea / evidence / inference / synthesis
Review within 24 hours — redo 3 similar items per miss type
Compare to st14 timed practice — note score trend
nodes.whyItMatters: This checkpoint proves the drill chain worked. Timed performance after focused review is the feedback loop that moves your R&W score.
practiceProblems: Bluebook R&W Module or Khan timed set — phone away, timer on.
Categorize every miss in your mistake log within 24 hours.
Pick one weak category from the log and schedule st26–st33 redo next week.

resources.title: Khan — Timed SAT Reading & Writing
resources.whyHelpful: Timed sets with instant review and skill tags.

resources.title: Bluebook timed practice
resources.whyHelpful: Official timing and short-passage format.

quiz.question: After a timed R&W checkpoint, highest-ROI next step is:
options: Log misses by category and drill each within 24 hours
quiz.explanation: Categorized review within 24 hours locks in fixes while mistakes are fresh.

quiz.question: During a timed R&W module, grammar items should generally be:
options: Answered quickly when you know the rule — save time for long passages
Done without reading the full sentence
quiz.explanation: Grammar items are often faster than inference items — bank time for harder reading questions.

nodes.name: Inequalities & Absolute Value Drill
nodes.description: One- and two-variable inequalities, compound inequalities, and absolute value equations/inequalities — Heart of Algebra at drill pace.
keyConcepts: Flip the inequality sign when multiplying or dividing by a negative
Compound inequalities: and vs or — graph on a number line
Absolute value |x| = a → x = a or x = -a
Desmos shading to backsolve inequality regions
nodes.whyItMatters: Inequality items look harder than they are — most are linear algebra with one extra rule. Drill the sign-flip reflex and absolute value splits so you don't burn time on setup.
practiceProblems: Solve: 3x - 7 > 2x + 5.
Solve: |2x - 3| = 7.
Khan: 15 linear inequality and absolute value SAT items — shade each on Desmos.

resources.title: Khan — Linear equations & inequalities
resources.whyHelpful: Heart of Algebra inequality drills with step-by-step solutions.

resources.title: Desmos Graphing Calculator
resources.whyHelpful: Graph inequalities and verify solution regions quickly.

quiz.question: When solving -2x > 6, you must:
options: Divide by -2 and keep the inequality direction
Divide by -2 and flip the inequality sign
quiz.explanation: Multiplying or dividing both sides by a negative reverses the inequality.

quiz.question: The equation |x - 4| = 3 has solutions:

nodes.name: Exponential Growth & Decay Drill
nodes.description: Exponential models, percent growth/decay, and comparing linear vs exponential change — Passport to Advanced Math.
keyConcepts: Exponential form: y = a(b)^t or y = a(1 ± r)^t
Doubling and half-life patterns in tables
Linear adds a constant; exponential multiplies by a constant factor
Percent growth/decay from word problems
nodes.whyItMatters: SAT exponentials reward pattern recognition in tables and graphs — not memorizing formulas. Spotting when a quantity doubles each step saves minutes vs setting up the wrong linear equation.
practiceProblems: A population starts at 500 and grows 8% per year. Write the model and find size after 3 years.
From a table, decide whether change is linear or exponential.
Khan: 12 exponential word problem SAT items — label base and rate on each.

resources.title: Khan — Exponential growth and decay
resources.whyHelpful: Passport to Advanced Math exponential models with SAT-style setups.

resources.title: Khan — SAT Math practice
resources.whyHelpful: Table and graph items showing doubling and percent change.

quiz.question: Which table most likely shows exponential growth?
quiz.explanation: Exponential change multiplies by a constant ratio, not a constant difference.

quiz.question: A quantity decreases by 20% each year. After 2 years it is what fraction of the start?
quiz.explanation: Two 20% decreases: 0.8 × 0.8 = 0.64 of the original.

nodes.name: Polynomial & Rational Expressions Drill
nodes.description: Polynomial add/multiply, factoring beyond quadratics, and simplifying rational expressions — equivalent-form traps on the SAT.
keyConcepts: Combine like terms; distribute carefully on polynomials
Factor common terms and recognize difference of squares
Simplify rational expressions — factor first, then cancel
Never cancel terms across + or - in a fraction
nodes.whyItMatters: Polynomial items often test whether you factor before simplifying. Canceling too early is the classic trap — drill factor-then-cancel until it's automatic.
practiceProblems: Expand and simplify: (x + 3)(x² - 2x + 1).
Simplify: (x² - 9) / (x - 3) for x ≠ 3.
Khan: 12 polynomial and rational expression SAT items — show factoring on every simplify.

resources.title: Khan — Polynomial operations
resources.whyHelpful: Add, multiply, and factor polynomials at SAT depth.

resources.title: Khan — SAT Math practice
resources.whyHelpful: Equivalent-form and simplify-expression items with trap distractors.

quiz.question: Simplify (x² - 4) / (x + 2) for x ≠ -2:
quiz.explanation: Factor x² - 4 = (x + 2)(x - 2), then cancel (x + 2).

quiz.question: A common SAT trap when simplifying rational expressions is:
options: Canceling terms across addition in a fraction
Multiplying top and bottom by the same factor
quiz.explanation: You can cancel factors, not separate terms joined by + or -.

nodes.name: Statistics & Scatterplots Drill
nodes.description: Mean, median, range, outliers, line of best fit, and interpreting slope/intercept in context — Problem Solving & Data Analysis.
keyConcepts: Mean vs median — outliers pull the mean, not always the median
Scatterplot trend: positive, negative, or no clear association
Line of best fit slope = rate of change in context
Desmos regression for table-to-line items
nodes.whyItMatters: Data items are reading questions disguised as math. One careful pass on units and what the axes mean beats recalculating everything from scratch.
practiceProblems: Dataset: 2, 3, 3, 8, 9. Find mean and median — which is larger and why?
From a scatterplot, describe the association and estimate slope meaning.
Khan: 12 statistics and scatterplot SAT items — write context for each slope.

resources.title: Khan — Data and statistics
resources.whyHelpful: Mean, median, and scatterplot interpretation drills.

resources.title: Desmos Graphing Calculator
resources.whyHelpful: Enter tables and fit regression lines for trend questions.

quiz.question: An outlier in a dataset most strongly affects:
quiz.explanation: The mean uses every value; a single extreme value pulls it more than the median.

quiz.question: On a scatterplot, a line of best fit with positive slope suggests:
options: As x increases, y tends to increase
As x increases, y tends to decrease
quiz.explanation: Positive slope means y increases on average as x increases.

nodes.name: Coordinate Geometry Drill
nodes.description: Distance and midpoint formulas, parallel/perpendicular slopes, and circle equations in the plane — Additional Topics in Math.
keyConcepts: Distance: √[(x₂-x₁)² + (y₂-y₁)²]
Midpoint: average of coordinates
Parallel lines → equal slopes; perpendicular → negative reciprocal slopes
Circle: (x - h)² + (y - k)² = r² — center (h, k), radius r
nodes.whyItMatters: Coordinate geometry connects algebra and visuals. Desmos plus the distance and circle formulas cover most SAT items without full geometry proofs.
practiceProblems: Find distance and midpoint between (1, 2) and (7, 10).
Line through (0, 3) perpendicular to y = 2x + 1 — write its equation.
Khan: 10 coordinate geometry SAT items — graph circles and lines in Desmos.

resources.title: Khan — Analytic geometry
resources.whyHelpful: Distance, midpoint, and circle equation drills.

resources.title: Desmos Graphing Calculator
resources.whyHelpful: Graph circles and verify distance between points visually.

quiz.question: The midpoint of (2, 4) and (8, 10) is:
A line perpendicular to y = 3x + 1 has slope:
quiz.explanation: Perpendicular slope is the negative reciprocal of 3 → -1/3.

nodes.name: Verb Tense & Parallel Structure Drill
nodes.description: Consistent tense, parallel lists and constructions, and perfect vs simple tense — Standard English Conventions under drill conditions.
keyConcepts: Keep verb tense consistent unless the sentence signals a time shift
Parallel structure: items in a list match grammatically (all -ing, all nouns, etc.)
Present perfect (has walked) vs simple past (walked) — ongoing vs completed
Correlative pairs: both/and, either/or, not only/but also must parallel
nodes.whyItMatters: Tense and parallelism errors are fast points when you know what to scan for. One read for verb form consistency catches traps that sound fine in casual speech.
practiceProblems: Fix: "She enjoys hiking, to swim, and riding bikes."
Fix: "By the time we arrived, the movie already started."
Khan: 15 verb tense and parallel structure SAT items — mark the non-parallel element.

resources.title: Khan — Form, structure, and sense
resources.whyHelpful: SEC form and structure drills with SAT-style underlined phrases.

resources.title: Khan — SAT Reading & Writing
resources.whyHelpful: Mix tense and parallelism with other grammar items.

quiz.question: Which revision fixes parallel structure: "He likes running, to jump, and swimming"?
options: He likes running, jumping, and swimming
He likes to run, to jump, and swimming
He likes running, jump, and swims
quiz.explanation: All three items should share the same form — gerunds: running, jumping, swimming.

quiz.question: Present perfect tense (has + past participle) often indicates:
options: Action completed at a specific past time only
Action linked to the present or continuing relevance
quiz.explanation: Present perfect connects past action to now — e.g., "has lived here for years."

nodes.name: Apostrophes & Possessives Drill
nodes.description: Possessive vs plural, its/it's, who's/whose, and they're/their/there in context — apostrophe traps on the Digital SAT.
keyConcepts: Possessive singular: add 's (the student's book)
Its = possessive; it's = it is — no apostrophe on possessive its
Who's = who is; whose = possessive
They're/their/there — contraction, possessive, location
nodes.whyItMatters: Apostrophe questions are pure pattern matching. Learn the five homophone pairs the SAT repeats and you pick up points without re-reading the whole passage.
practiceProblems: Choose correctly: "The team celebrated ___ victory after ___ practice."
Fix: "Its a common mistake to confuse its and it's."
Khan: 15 apostrophe and possessive SAT items — name the rule for each miss.

resources.title: Khan — Punctuation and form
resources.whyHelpful: Apostrophe and possessive drills in official SAT style.

resources.title: Bluebook — Practice Tests
resources.whyHelpful: Short grammar stems testing apostrophe traps in the real interface.

quiz.question: Which is correct?
quiz.explanation: Possessive its has no apostrophe; it's always means it is.

quiz.question: "Who's responsible for the project?" uses who's as:
quiz.explanation: Who's = who is; whose shows possession (whose project).

nodes.name: Cross-Text Connections Drill
nodes.description: Compare two short texts — agree/disagree, shared claim, and differences in evidence — Craft and Structure on the Digital SAT.
keyConcepts: Identify each author's main claim before comparing
Agree on topic ≠ agree on conclusion — check the claim, not just the subject
Evidence differences: what each author cites to support their view
Digital SAT paired-passage format — read Text 1, then Text 2, then compare
nodes.whyItMatters: Cross-text items test whether you can hold two arguments in mind at once — same skill as comparing two news takes on a trade. Drill the compare-after-summarize workflow.
practiceProblems: Two texts on the same topic: write one sentence each author's claim, then one sentence on how they differ.
For one cross-text set, explain why a "both agree" trap answer is wrong.
Khan: 10 cross-text connection SAT items — summarize each text in 10 words first.

resources.title: Khan — Craft and structure
resources.whyHelpful: Cross-text connections and paired-passage comparison drills.

resources.title: Bluebook — Practice Tests
resources.whyHelpful: Official paired-passage format matches test day.

quiz.question: On a cross-text question, the best first step is:
options: Summarize each author's main claim before comparing
Pick the answer that mentions both texts equally
quiz.explanation: You need each author's claim clear before evaluating agreement or disagreement.

quiz.question: Two texts on climate policy that reach opposite conclusions:
options: May share a topic but differ in claims or evidence
Must be written by the same author
quiz.explanation: Same topic, different claims or evidence — classic cross-text setup.

nodes.name: Text Structure & Organization Drill
nodes.description: Passage purpose, paragraph function, and logical sequence of sentences — Craft and Structure organization items.
keyConcepts: Passage purpose: inform, argue, narrate, compare — match answer to whole text
Paragraph function: introduction, evidence, counterargument, conclusion
Sentence placement: new sentence must connect to before and after
Logical sequence — chronological, cause-effect, or general-to-specific
nodes.whyItMatters: Structure questions reward outline thinking. If you can name what each paragraph does, placement and purpose items become quick instead of guesswork.
practiceProblems: One passage: label each paragraph's function in one phrase.
One "best placement" item: explain why the chosen spot fits both neighbors.
Khan: 10 text structure and organization SAT items.

resources.title: Khan — Craft and structure
resources.whyHelpful: Text structure, purpose, and sentence placement drills.

resources.title: Bluebook — Practice Tests
resources.whyHelpful: Official "best placement" and structure-of-argument questions.

quiz.question: A "best placement" question asks where a new sentence should go. It must:
options: Connect logically to the sentences before and after the gap
Be placed in the middle always
Repeat the thesis word for word
quiz.explanation: The inserted sentence must flow with both neighboring sentences.

quiz.question: A paragraph that presents an opposing view and then refutes it is likely:
quiz.explanation: Acknowledging then refuting opposition is a common argumentative structure.

nodes.name: Charts & Quantitative R&W Drill
nodes.description: Read axes and units, match charts to claims, and find data-supported inferences — Information and Ideas with quantitative evidence.
keyConcepts: Read axis labels, units, and scale before the question
Claim must match what the data actually shows — not exaggeration
Table vs graph: same data, different presentation — check both
Reject answers that confuse correlation with causation
nodes.whyItMatters: Quantitative R&W items punish skimming. Verifying units and scale takes seconds and prevents picking answers that sound right but misread the chart.
practiceProblems: One graph + passage set: write what the y-axis measures and its units before answering.
For three wrong choices, note whether each misreads scale, units, or trend.
Khan: 10 charts and quantitative evidence SAT items — check units first every time.

resources.title: Khan — Information and ideas
resources.whyHelpful: Graph and table paired with short passage — command of quantitative evidence.

resources.title: Bluebook — Practice Tests
resources.whyHelpful: Official graph/table R&W items in the digital format.

quiz.question: Before answering a chart question, you should first:
options: Check axis labels, units, and what each variable represents
Estimate without looking at the scale
quiz.explanation: Units and scale errors are the most common trap — verify before interpreting.

quiz.question: A valid inference from a chart must:
options: Go beyond the data to explain why
Be directly supported by the displayed data
Use outside knowledge not in the graph
quiz.explanation: The answer must follow from what the chart or table actually shows.

nodes.name: Volume & Advanced Geometry Drill
nodes.description: 3D volume and surface area, composite figures, and arc/sector extensions — Additional Topics building on st23 and st40.
keyConcepts: Volume formulas: prism, cylinder, cone, sphere — know what each dimension means
Surface area: net thinking and labeled diagrams
Composite figures — split into familiar shapes, add or subtract
Arc length and sector area from central angle and radius
nodes.whyItMatters: Geometry items are fewer but often feel unfamiliar if you missed class days. Volume and composite-figure drills prevent easy points from slipping away on labeled diagrams.
practiceProblems: Find volume of a cylinder with r = 3 and h = 10 — include units.
A composite figure: rectangle with semicircle on top — find total area.
Khan: 12 volume and advanced geometry SAT items — sketch each diagram before calculating.

resources.title: Khan — Geometry SAT practice
resources.whyHelpful: Additional Topics geometry and measurement at SAT depth.

resources.title: Khan — Solid geometry
resources.whyHelpful: Volume and surface area with step-by-step solutions.

quiz.question: Volume of a cylinder is V = πr²h. If radius doubles and height stays the same, volume:
quiz.explanation: Volume depends on r² — doubling r multiplies volume by 4.

quiz.question: For a composite figure on the SAT, the best first step is:
options: Split into familiar shapes and label every dimension
quiz.explanation: Decompose the figure and verify units before calculating.

nodes.name: Nonlinear Graphs & Systems Drill
nodes.description: Parabola-line intersections, number of solutions, and graph-based systems — Passport to Advanced Math at drill pace.
keyConcepts: Line and parabola: 0, 1, or 2 intersection points
Set equations equal to find intersection x-values
Discriminant intuition: b² - 4ac tells you solution count for quadratics
Desmos: graph both curves and count intersection points
nodes.whyItMatters: "How many solutions" items reward graph thinking over long algebra. Desmos plus intersection count beats grinding the quadratic formula under time pressure.
practiceProblems: How many solutions does y = x² and y = x + 2 have? Verify in Desmos.
Find intersection points of y = x² - 4 and y = 2x.
Khan: 12 nonlinear systems SAT items — graph before solving algebraically.

resources.title: Khan — Nonlinear equations and systems
resources.whyHelpful: Quadratic and system items with SAT-style intersection questions.

resources.title: Desmos Graphing Calculator
resources.whyHelpful: Graph parabolas and lines to count and verify intersections.

quiz.question: A line and a parabola can intersect at:
quiz.explanation: A line can miss, touch once (tangent), or cross a parabola twice.

quiz.question: For "how many solutions" questions, Desmos helps by:
options: Replacing the need to read the question
Letting you count intersection points visually
quiz.explanation: Graph both equations and count where they cross — fast sanity check.

nodes.name: Hard Word Problems — Multi-Step Setup
nodes.description: Multi-representation setup: table → equation → graph. Rate, work, and mixture problems at SAT pace.
keyConcepts: Define the variable in one sentence before writing an equation
Rate × time = distance; work-rate sums for combined jobs
Table to equation: identify what changes at a constant rate
Mark the setup step — most misses are wrong models, not arithmetic
nodes.whyItMatters: Long SAT stems test whether you can translate a story into math once. Drilling the setup step — not the calculation — is what separates fast scores from stuck ones.
practiceProblems: Two pipes fill a tank at different rates — write combined-rate equation for time to fill.
From a table of distance vs time, write the linear model and interpret slope.
Khan: 12 multi-step word problem SAT items — write setup only before checking answers.

resources.title: Khan — SAT Math word problems
resources.whyHelpful: Multi-step modeling across Heart of Algebra and PSDA.

resources.title: Desmos Graphing Calculator
resources.whyHelpful: Graph table data to verify linear models and intercepts.

quiz.question: On a long word problem, the highest-ROI first step is:
options: Define what x represents in one clear sentence
quiz.explanation: A clear variable definition prevents wrong-equation traps on complex stems.

quiz.question: If two workers complete a job together in 3 hours, their combined rate is:
quiz.explanation: Rates add when working together: 1/t₁ + 1/t₂ = 1/t_combined.

nodes.name: Math Mistake-Log Retarget Drill
nodes.description: Pull top 3 miss categories from your mistake log; untimed redo plus 3 similar items per category before the half-test checkpoint.
keyConcepts: Rank miss categories from st25 and st36–st48 logs
Untimed redo of original misses — no notes, fresh attempt
3 similar Khan items per weak category
No new topics — retarget what already cost you points
nodes.whyItMatters: The half-test checkpoint is only useful if you fixed your actual weak spots first. Retargeting from your log beats random mixed practice.
practiceProblems: List your top 3 Math miss categories from st25 and recent drills.
Redo each original miss untimed, then 3 similar Khan questions per category.
Bluebook: untimed review of missed item types from st25 log.

resources.title: Khan — SAT Math practice
resources.whyHelpful: Filter by weak domain: Algebra, PSDA, Advanced Math, or Geometry.

resources.title: Bluebook — Practice Tests
resources.whyHelpful: Untimed review of missed item types in the real interface.

quiz.question: Before a half-test checkpoint, mistake-log retarget should focus on:
options: Your top 3 actual miss categories from prior checkpoints
Only questions you already got right
quiz.explanation: Retarget categories that already cost points — not random drills.

quiz.question: After redoing a miss untimed, the best follow-up is:
options: 3 similar items in the same category without notes
Delete the miss from your log
quiz.explanation: Similar items in the same category lock in the fix while the pattern is fresh.

nodes.name: Math — Bluebook Half-Test Checkpoint
nodes.description: Timed Math modules (Module 1 + Module 2 or equivalent half-test block). Log misses by domain within 24 hours — capstone of the st36–st50 drill chain.
keyConcepts: Timed both Math modules under test-like conditions
Tag misses: algebra / PSDA / advanced / geometry
Review within 24 hours — redo 3 similar items per miss type
Compare to st25 timed checkpoint — note score trend
nodes.whyItMatters: This half-test checkpoint simulates real section pressure after round-2 drills. Comparing to st25 shows whether your retargeting worked — the feedback loop that moves your Math score.
practiceProblems: Bluebook timed Math Module 1 + Module 2 — phone away, timer on.
Categorize every miss in your mistake log within 24 hours.
Compare raw score and timing to st25 — note which domains improved.

resources.title: Khan — Timed SAT Math
resources.whyHelpful: Timed sets mirroring module length if Bluebook isn't available.

resources.title: Bluebook timed practice
resources.whyHelpful: Official timing, Desmos, and adaptive module feel for both Math modules.

quiz.question: After a Math half-test checkpoint, highest-ROI next step is:
options: Log misses by domain and drill each within 24 hours
quiz.explanation: Categorized review within 24 hours locks in fixes while mistakes are fresh.

quiz.question: A Math half-test checkpoint should be compared to:
options: st25 timed checkpoint — note score and timing trend
quiz.explanation: st25 is your baseline timed Math checkpoint — track improvement from there.

nodes.name: Conciseness & Redundancy Drill
nodes.description: Delete redundancy, choose the shortest grammatically correct option — Expression of Ideas under drill conditions.
keyConcepts: Shortest choice must still be grammatically complete and clear
Redundant pairs: "past history," "combine together," "each individual"
Do not sacrifice necessary meaning or clarity for brevity
Read the full sentence — concision traps often drop a required word
nodes.whyItMatters: Concision items are quick points when you know the pattern: if two choices say the same thing, pick the shorter one that still works. Drill the redundancy scan before timed modules.
practiceProblems: Fix: "The reason is because the team was tired."
Fix: "They merged together the two departments."
Khan: 15 concision and redundancy SAT items — mark the redundant phrase each time.

resources.title: Khan — Expression of ideas
resources.whyHelpful: Concision drills with "most effectively" revision stems.

resources.title: Bluebook — Practice Tests
resources.whyHelpful: Official concise-revision items in the digital format.

quiz.question: Which phrase is redundant?
quiz.explanation: History is already in the past — "past history" repeats the idea.

quiz.question: On a concision question, the correct answer must be:
options: The shortest option regardless of grammar
The shortest option that is grammatically correct and preserves meaning
The option with the most adjectives
quiz.explanation: Concision means efficient, not incomplete — grammar and clarity still matter.

nodes.name: Author's Tone & Style Drill
nodes.description: Tone words, attitude, and stylistic choices — reject extreme or off-tone options on Craft and Structure items.
keyConcepts: Tone = author's attitude toward the subject (neutral, skeptical, admiring, etc.)
Style clues: word choice, sentence length, formal vs informal register
Reject answers that are too extreme unless the passage supports them
Vocabulary in context — pick the word that matches register, not just definition
nodes.whyItMatters: Tone items test whether you read for attitude, not just facts. One pass for word choice and overall stance beats picking answers that sound sophisticated but misread the author.
practiceProblems: One humanities passage: write one word for tone and one sentence of evidence.
One science passage: distinguish neutral reporting from persuasive language.
Khan: 12 tone and style SAT items — eliminate extreme choices first.

resources.title: Khan — Craft and structure
resources.whyHelpful: Words in context, tone, and register drills.

resources.title: Bluebook — Practice Tests
resources.whyHelpful: Official tone and vocabulary-in-context items.

quiz.question: On a tone question, "skeptical" means the author:
quiz.explanation: Skeptical tone shows doubt or questioning — look for hedging or counterevidence.

quiz.question: A common tone trap is choosing an answer that is:
options: Supported by specific word choices in the passage
More extreme than the passage language supports
Neutral when the passage is neutral
quiz.explanation: SAT tone answers match the passage's degree — not stronger than the text allows.

nodes.name: Form, Structure & Sense — Mixed Grammar
nodes.description: Combined Standard English Conventions: boundaries plus form/structure in one timed-style set — grammar under module pacing.
keyConcepts: Mix punctuation (commas, semicolons, dashes) with form/structure (tense, agreement, parallelism)
Read the full sentence — boundaries and verb form often interact
Timed-style pacing: ~45–60 seconds per grammar item
No new rules — combine st26–st28 and st41–st42 patterns
nodes.whyItMatters: Real R&W modules switch grammar subtypes fast. Mixed SEC drills build the reflex to scan for boundaries and form in one pass — same rhythm as test day.
practiceProblems: Khan: 20 mixed SEC items (punctuation + agreement + tense) under a 15-minute timer.
For each miss, name both the rule and the sentence part that triggered it.
Redo 5 boundary items and 5 form/structure items from your mistake log.

resources.title: Khan — Standard English Conventions
resources.whyHelpful: Full SEC mix: boundaries, form, structure, and sense.

resources.title: Bluebook — Practice Tests
resources.whyHelpful: Rapid grammar items under module pacing.

quiz.question: Mixed SEC practice should mimic test day by:
options: Doing only one grammar subtype per session
Switching boundary and form/structure items under time pressure
quiz.explanation: Modules mix SEC subtypes — practice with pacing and variety.

quiz.question: When a sentence has both a comma and a verb error, fix:
options: The issue the question underlines or asks about
quiz.explanation: Answer what the item tests — underline or prompt tells you the target.

nodes.name: R&W Mistake-Log Retarget & Mixed Review
nodes.description: Untimed 20-question mix from st41–st53 categories. Tag every miss before the R&W half-test checkpoint.
keyConcepts: Mix grammar, craft, info, and expression from round-2 drills
Tag each miss: concision / tone / SEC / cross-text / charts / etc.
Redo similar Khan questions for every miss
No new topics — consolidate st41–st53 before st55
nodes.whyItMatters: Single-skill drills build accuracy; mixed retarget review reveals what you forget when question types switch — exactly what a half-test module feels like.
practiceProblems: Khan: 20-question untimed mixed R&W set spanning st41–st53 topics.
For each miss, write one sentence: category + what fix.
Bluebook: untimed R&W module review — categorize misses before st55.

resources.title: Khan — SAT Reading & Writing
resources.whyHelpful: Mixed official-style sets with skill labels.

resources.title: Bluebook — Practice Tests
resources.whyHelpful: Untimed module review in the real test interface.

quiz.question: Before a R&W half-test checkpoint, mixed review should be:
options: Untimed with every miss tagged by skill category
quiz.explanation: Tagged untimed review targets weak spots before the clock turns on.

quiz.question: R&W mistake-log retarget should pull misses from:
quiz.explanation: Round-2 categories plus st35 baseline show what still costs points.

nodes.name: R&W — Bluebook Half-Test Checkpoint
nodes.description: Timed R&W modules (Module 1 + Module 2 or equivalent half-test block). Log misses by category within 24 hours — capstone of the st41–st55 drill chain.
keyConcepts: Timed both R&W modules under test-like conditions
Tag misses: grammar / craft / info / expression
Review within 24 hours — redo 3 similar items per miss type
Compare to st35 timed checkpoint — note score trend
nodes.whyItMatters: This half-test checkpoint simulates real section pressure after round-2 R&W drills. Comparing to st35 shows whether your retargeting worked — the feedback loop that moves your R&W score.
practiceProblems: Bluebook timed R&W Module 1 + Module 2 — phone away, timer on.
Categorize every miss in your mistake log within 24 hours.
Compare raw score and timing to st35 — note which categories improved.

resources.title: Khan — Timed SAT Reading & Writing
resources.whyHelpful: Timed sets mirroring module length if Bluebook isn't available.

resources.title: Bluebook timed practice
resources.whyHelpful: Official timing and adaptive module feel for both R&W modules.

quiz.question: After a R&W half-test checkpoint, highest-ROI next step is:
options: Log misses by category and drill each within 24 hours
quiz.explanation: Categorized review within 24 hours locks in fixes while mistakes are fresh.

quiz.question: A R&W half-test checkpoint should be compared to:
options: st35 timed checkpoint — note score and timing trend
quiz.explanation: st35 is your baseline timed R&W checkpoint — track improvement from there.
